Dr. Michael C. Cifone
Founding Executive Director & President
JULY 2026
Foreword

From the Executive Director

The Society for UAP Studies was founded on a conviction that the UAP problem is, at its core, a scientific and epistemological problem of the first order — and that it deserves the same institutional seriousness, methodological rigor, and long-term commitment that any other fundamental scientific frontier commands.

Over the past eighteen months, I have watched that conviction take institutional form in ways that would have seemed ambitious when we began. Today, SUAPS is an independent, functioning, internationally networked learned society with an active research portfolio, a growing publications program, a public educational presence that has reached audiences in the hundreds of thousands, and a governance architecture that is being progressively hardened to support the kind of serious, sustained work that this emerging field demands.

This document is my attempt to give you — our Board of Advisors, our Society Affiliates, our partners, and our broader community of supporters — a clear and honest account of where we stand. It follows in the spirit of the commitment I made when we formed the Advisory Council: to ensure that all of you receive a regular, substantive view of what the organization is doing and where we are headed, not simply the promotional highlights but the full picture, including the constraints and the open questions.

2026 has been a year of deliberate consolidation and strategic re-anchoring. We entered the year carrying the momentum of a successful second annual conference, two landmark $50,000 unrestricted donations, and a public profile that had expanded well beyond the UAP research community. We have spent H1 ensuring that the organizational infrastructure is adequate to the ambitions we have already set in motion, and we are now positioned to enter H2 with renewed strategic focus on the research and educational work that defines who we are.

The short version: we are building something that has not quite existed before, and we are doing it carefully.

Dr. Michael C. Cifone
Founding Executive Director & President · Society for UAP Studies
01

Who We Are and What We Are Building

The Society for UAP Studies is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit learned society organized around a single animating conviction: that the UAP problem — the persistent encounter of credible observers with phenomena that appear to resist systematic scientific characterization — is not merely a policy question, a cultural phenomenon, or a curiosity for enthusiasts, but a genuine frontier problem in the philosophy and practice of scientific and scholarly inquiry, and that it deserves the full institutional apparatus — rigorous peer review, disciplinary standards, methodological debate, educational programs, and a long-term institutional home — that serious science requires.

We are not a disclosure advocacy group, not a popular UAP media outlet, and not a UFO organization in the traditional sense. We are building the independent intellectual and institutional foundations for UAP Studies as a legitimate, evidence-based academic discipline that operates apart from the emerging special interests that increasingly dominate public discourse on UAP.

Three strategic pillars structure our work

I
Learned & Professional Society

Flagship research initiatives, academic conferences and workshops, professional colloquia, distinguished lecture series, intensive advanced seminars, peer-reviewed academic publications (flagship journal: Limina — The Journal of UAP Studies), and ethics & standards committees.

II
INTERNAL RESEARCH PROJECTS & GOVERNANCE

AURA is our basic research project lifecycle and governance framework for donor-directed and externally-received and approved project grants, mirrored internally for SUAPS' own research initiatives — the pipeline through which high-quality, methodologically sound UAP research is conceptualized, managed, executed, funded, reported, and finally synthesized. Our internal research is offered as foundational for the field.

III
Education & Public Outreach

Public outreach events (the livestreamed Lecture Series, the Community Reading Circle), responsible media initiatives, the bimonthly SUAPS Newsletter, and membership offerings — translating scholarly research into learning experiences and engagement opportunities for both academic and public audiences.

02

Leadership and Team

SUAPS is directed by a genuinely international team — leaders and advisors working from ten countries across five continents, a distribution that reflects both the global nature of the UAP question and our conviction that a serious learned society must be built from the widest possible community of qualified minds. Authority rests with the Board of Directors; the Advisory Council provides strategic and ethical steering; and a division of departmental directors carries the Society's programmatic and operational work.

The Board of Advisors

Beyond the standing Council, the Society's Board of Advisors comprises nearly forty scholars and scientists across five continents — drawn from institutions including the University of Chicago, Penn State, SUNY Albany, University College London, Wellesley College, Simon Fraser University, and the University of Queensland, among many others. The full roster is maintained at societyforuapstudies.org.

03

Where We Stand — The 2026 Picture

3
Active research initiatives (SIRIs)

3.1  H1 2026: Consolidating the Foundation

The first half of 2026 has been deliberately framed as an Infrastructure Consolidation Phase — not a retreat from mission, but a time-bounded commitment to ensuring that the organizational backbone is adequate to sustain what we are building. In practical terms, this has meant completing and adopting coherent financial controls and DDRG governance, clarifying operational ownership across the organization, and ensuring that the processes by which we make commitments, manage funds, and deliver on our obligations meet the standards appropriate to a serious, growing academic nonprofit.

The consolidation phase was not an interruption of programmatic work — programs have continued throughout — but it has required the discipline to prioritize infrastructure-critical tasks alongside delivery, and to resist scaling new commitments faster than our administrative capacity to support them. As H2 opens, the foundation is materially stronger, and the pivot to renewed strategic focus on our core research and educational programs is underway.

3.2  Organizational Highlights — 2025 Into 2026

  • —The Second Annual SUAPS Conference, hosted at a major German university (December 2025), with front-page USA Today coverage, a feature in The New Yorker, and reporting in Space.com — signals of the field's growing legitimacy and of SUAPS's position as a credible institutional home for serious UAP scholarship.
  • —Limina Vol. 2 published (December 2025); Vol. 3 on track for Spring/Summer 2026. Limina continues to establish SUAPS's editorial credibility in the academic space.
  • —Three major unrestricted donations of $50,000 each — the first supporting core programs and operations with a deliberate strategic reserve, the second received in January 2026, giving SUAPS the capacity to launch and sustain the projects in its research portfolio and underwrite the institutional work that makes that portfolio possible. And the third enabling a strategic investment into institutional partnerships and key fellowships for graduate students, and a sprint to our next annual conference in December 2026, planned for Porto, Portugal.
  • —AURA fully operational: formal DDRG templates, reporting requirements, and an operating model in place; the Baltic Sea Anomaly Phase 1 expedition successfully executed and reported — SUAPS's first major donor-directed research project completed under the new governance framework.
  • —The UAP Studies Public Seminar Series launched (February 2026): approximately 62,000 YouTube subscribers, 1,500 LinkedIn followers, and a viral short crossing one million views — well beyond the initial projections for an academic programming initiative.
  • —Strategic Institutional Research Initiatives (SIRIs): all three foundational research programs — The Empirical Weird, Forensic Methodologies, and UAP Standards of Evidence — are live and progressing.
04

Research — The Strategic Institutional Research Initiatives

The "SIRIs" are SUAPS's most significant intellectual investments: the foundational research programs that define what kind of knowledge organization we are, and that position SUAPS to contribute original, rigorous thinking to the most consequential open questions in the scientific study of UAP. All three are active and forward-moving, each at a distinct stage of development.

4.1  The Empirical Weird Initiative

To bring scholarly and scientific discipline to UAP Studies precisely when it gets weird — confronting abductions, entity encounters, altered states, and cross-domain anomalous phenomena directly and without flinching. The 'grey' literature has documented these experiences for decades; they deserve rigorous treatment.

The Empirical Weird Initiative is our most philosophically and methodologically ambitious research program to date. It addresses a specific and persistent gap in UAP Studies: the phenomena that are most frequently reported by credible observers — entity encounters, altered states, psychophysical events, 'high strangeness' in its many forms — are precisely the phenomena that both popular discourse sensationalizes and academic discourse reflexively brackets, dismisses or traps in metaphysical doctrines of the past. Neither response constitutes inquiry. The opening forth of that inquiry leads us to embrace a radical empiricism, in the spirit of William James, combined with the radical Enlightenment vision of Spinoza.

Phase 1 of the initiative concluded with a structured retreat at Fermil de Basto, Portugal (28 May – 1 June 2026), led by Dr. Michael Cifone and Dr. Michael Silberstein, hosted locally by SUAPS Director of Regional Outreach for Europe, Francisco Mourão Corrêa, and dedicated to the memory of the late philosopher Stephen Braude, whose meticulous documentation of anomalous phenomena represents a foundational contribution to the kind of rigorous empirical work the initiative aims to advance. The retreat brought together an interdisciplinary group committed to three foundational intellectual commitments:

  • —A radical empiricist methodology — following William James's principle of 'putting experience first,' refusing to dismiss anomalous phenomena as culturally constructed before they have been empirically examined on their own terms.
  • —Explicit identification and avoidance of the traps that have undermined previous attempts at this inquiry: both extreme metaphysics (reductive physicalism and absolute idealism alike) and academic 'safety' in the form of social/cultural reductionism.
  • —Development of genuinely new ontological and methodological frameworks adequate to the phenomena — including a Spinoza-motivated Neutral Monism as a candidate framework for anomalous phenomena that exceed the mind/matter distinction that is rich enough to allow for the positing of concrete models of the empirical data collected under the empiricist methodology currently being developed.

Phase 2 is now in planning, and will include a major field deployment, a working paper synthesizing methodological outcomes, and a workshop extending the initiative to a broader interdisciplinary network. Phase 3 will include a major international Symposium on our work, which will include the larger international network of engaged scholars, a special issue of Limina dedicated to the empirical weird, and a final report on the project team's findings and future directions.

4.2  The Forensic Methodologies Initiative

The Forensic Methodologies SIRI addresses one of the most fundamental technical gaps in the UAP evidence base: the absence of a truly and meaningfully rigorous, cross-disciplinary forensic methodology for the analysis of UAP physical evidence. The initiative conducts a systematic critical review of all prior forensic approaches in the UAP literature — from the Condon Study and BAAS/Bigelow field studies, through Nolan et al. (2022) and key comparative physical events (Carancas, Chelyabinsk) — before constructing and recommending a new methodological framework grounded in the established standards and best practices of the forensic sciences.

The initiative is building toward the Forensic Methodology Working Group Brazil Pilot, developed in connection with SUAPS's Latin America engagement program under its current Director for Regional Outreach Esteban Corio, and is currently producing a working paper that will serve as the first substantive output of the initiative in the form of an institutional research White Paper. Lead by a research team that includes Dr. Josh Pierson, SUAPS Advisory Board member and SCU contributing member, as well as Limina subject area editor Dr. Eric Halford, who is an expert in criminology and law enforcement, The core intellectual move is treating UAP physical evidence analysis not as a special case requiring special pleading but as a domain that can and should be held to the rigorous standards of field forensics — which requires first understanding exactly how those standards have and have not been applied in the historical literature. Given the recent move by the Brazilian government to formally recognize "ufology" as a profession, we see our collaborative work in this SIRI as providing strategic opportunity for reshaping the classical, quasi-professional tradition of UFO investigations into a professionalized form: a true forensic science of UAP in academic and professional conversation with existing systematic practical and theoretical forensics. The deeper question will be what to do with the excessive remainder: cases that seem to drift, at least for some of the witnesses, into more spiritual territory. Here is where the SUAPS SIRI ecosystem works profoundly synergistically: such cases can be brought into relation with our "Empirical Weird" project and handled with the sophistication, rigor and care they require.

4.3  UAP Standards of Evidence Initiative

The Standards of Evidence SIRI is the most foundational of the three: a radically interdisciplinary initiative aimed at clarifying what constitutes good evidence, robust argument, credible claim, and a viable pathway from data to empirically-grounded theoretical understanding in the scientific study of UAP. The initiative was modeled explicitly on the successful work of the astrobiology community in developing standards of evidence for the detection of extraterrestrial life — work that established shared epistemic norms allowing productive scientific argument.

The initiative submitted a proposal to the Lorentz Center (University of Leiden, Netherlands) — one of the world's leading venues for high-intensity interdisciplinary scientific workshops — for a dedicated workshop on UAP standards of evidence. The Lorentz Center ultimately declined to move forward after a substantial revision to our project was submitted. This is a setback but not a defeat: the proposal process generated a substantially developed concept document, a defined participant network (of over 40 scholars and scientists from around the world), and a clear intellectual agenda. The initiative is now in full rescoping mode, evaluating alternative venues and formats, with the working group remaining active and the deliverables — a standards of evidence white paper and a community report — firmly on the table. A meeting in Lisbon, Portugal during the August AstroAccel Summit is planned, where the group hopes to attract support for this impactful initiative.

05

Partnerships and Global Engagement

Increasingly, SUAPS does its most consequential work through partnership. Over the course of 2026 the Society has extended its reach across the Americas and Europe — through institutional collaborations with aligned research organizations, sponsored public events at major universities, and a developing fellowship program aimed at the next generation of scholars. This is how an emerging discipline builds itself: not in isolation, but in disciplined conversation with the best of the existing intellectual and institutional landscape.

5.1  Institutional Partnerships

Scientific Coalition for UAP Studies (SCU). Limina and SUAPS are collaborating with an editorial team from the SCU on a special conference-proceedings volume of essays tied to the SCU's July annual meeting — a concrete step toward shared publishing infrastructure and common standards across the field's most serious institutions.

Yale University. In parallel, SUAPS is developing a limited partnership with several departments at Yale University, in support of a forthcoming event closely aligned with the Empirical Weird Initiative. We are proud to work with their team on such a key scholarly event.

5.2  Regional Outreach & Sponsored Events

  • —New York, USA. SUAPS sponsored a May event at the Ralph Bunche Center for International Affairs at the CUNY Graduate Center, featuring a well-attended public talk by Leslie Kean. Outgoing director of the Center and newly-appointed SUAPS Advisory Board member Dr. Eli Khartney organized the event to much success, which was attended by over 200 people from the local New York City area.
  • —Brazil. In early March, SUAPS sponsored a one-day public lecture series at which Executive Director Michael Cifone delivered a well-received lecture, along with COO Rafael Di Carlantonio's sophisticated lecture (in Portuguese) on navigating the institutional challenges the UAP subjects presents given its inherent intellectual complexity.
  • —Argentina. The Society's sponsorship of the Argentina Congreso deepened SUAPS's engagement with the large and complex Latin American research community and anchors an expanding regional academic network under our LATAM outreach directorate. Mr. Corio delivered to wide acclaim (in Spanish) a lecture developed from Dr. Cifone's ‘Two Dogmas’ talk, first given for the Society's Public Seminar Series in April.

5.3  Fellowships — Responsible Media & Journalism

SUAPS is developing a Fellowship in Responsible Media and Journalism, working with universities and their graduate students to support projects of strategic — and intellectual and ethical — importance for the field. The fellowship, which the Society hopes to partner with other serious UAP-focused organizations to match funding, reflects a conviction that the quality of public understanding of UAP depends on the quality of the people trained to communicate it; it is among the initiatives enabled by the Society's most recent unrestricted gift.

06

Education, Publications, and Community Programs

6.1  The UAP Studies Public Seminar Series

The Seminar Series is SUAPS's most publicly visible educational program and our primary driver of community engagement. Launched in February 2026 under the tagline 'Foundations of a New Field,' the series has built a meaningful audience base in a remarkably short time: approximately 62,000 YouTube subscribers, 1,500 LinkedIn followers, and a viral short that crossed one million views. These numbers reflect genuine public appetite for serious, credible treatment of UAP as a scientific and scholarly question — the appetite that SUAPS exists to address.

The series is pausing for June and July to enable a structured, well-prepared relaunch in August, under the new project leadership of Dr. Maya Cowan (Community Programs & Public Engagement Manager), with a refined audience framing — the educated general public — and a clearer conversion path from viewer to member with the help of our marketing team under the senior advisement of Deb West (who also works with the SCU). The relaunch will operate on a bi-weekly schedule, with teaser content freely available on YouTube and full lectures accessible through the SUAPS website for members and supporters.

Longer horizon: each lecturer in the series will later be invited to contribute a chapter to what will become a collectively authored UAP Studies textbook — a deliberate design that seeds a major future publication from what is, on the surface, a public education program.

6.2  Foundations of UAP Studies — College Credit Course

SUAPS is preparing to launch what we believe to be the first college-credit undergraduate course dedicated specifically to UAP Studies as an emerging interdisciplinary academic discipline — moving beyond the cultural and humanistic or purely natural scientific, aerospatial or national security framings that have characterized most prior coursework, in a concerted effort to address the harder and more vexing epistemological and empirical questions at the center of the field. Offered in cooperation with a U.S. liberal arts college, the course will carry college credit and is scheduled to launch in January 2027, with a marketing campaign beginning in August. It is designed to serve both enrolled undergraduate students and adult learners seeking substantive engagement with an emerging scientific frontier.

6.3  Publications — Limina and SUAPS Press

Limina Vol. 3 is on track for Spring/Summer 2026 publication (10 July release), continuing the journal's role as the primary peer-reviewed venue for UAP scholarship in the English-speaking world. A special issue on the empirical weird is in early pre-planning as a Phase 3 deliverable of the Empirical Weird SIRI. Additional special issues and collaborative ventures are planned, most immediately with an editorial team from the Scientific Coalition for UAP Studies to do a special conference proceedings volume of essays related to their upcoming July annual conference.

SUAPS Press has announced its inaugural translation project: a new English edition of Dr. Bertrand Méheust's 1978 classic Science-fiction et soucoupes volantes, accompanied by commissioned introductory and commentary essays that situate the work within the current state of UAP Studies. The translation, the first for this classic and seminal French text, is on track for Spring 2027 release — a signal of SUAPS's commitment to making the international history of the field's intellectual development accessible to English-speaking scholars and students.

6.4  Community and Academic Programs

Annual SUAPS Conference
Planning underway for the third annual conference; planning committee active. December 2026 target date, with location set for a major university in Porto, Portugal.
Colloquia Series
Ongoing monthly programming; coordination with international academic networks.
Community Reading Circle
Active public engagement with the scholarly literature.
Latin America Regional Outreach
Following the Brazil engagement and Argentina UFO Congress sponsorship — developing a regional academic network.
07

Governance and Organizational Maturity

One of the most important things happening at SUAPS in 2026 is less visible than our publications or public programs, but is in many ways more consequential for the organization's long-term credibility and sustainability: we are becoming, deliberately and step by step, a more institutionally mature organization.

For those who have followed SUAPS since the beginning, this means investing in the governance and operational infrastructure that allows our research and educational work to proceed without the ad-hoc exception-making, process debt, and key-person dependency that are endemic to young, fast-moving nonprofits. We are not trying to become bureaucratic — we are trying to become trustworthy, in the full institutional sense: reliable in our commitments, predictable in our processes, and accountable in our use of the resources our supporters have entrusted to us.

Key governance developments in 2026

  • —Advisory Council (AC) formalization: the AC is continuing to develop as SUAPS's primary strategic steering body, distinct from the broader Board of Advisors. Governance materials, including sharper role definitions, and onboarding frameworks are being completed while key strategic agenda items (like AI & Experiencer Policies, and Professional Membership framework) are under submission for council deliberation.
  • —DDRG governance: formal templates, reporting requirements, and an operating model for Donor-Directed Research Grants are now in place, providing donors with clear documentation, standard terms, and predictable reporting.
  • —Financial controls: board and finance oversight thresholds for major commitments are being formally documented; basic accounting hygiene is being regularized.
  • —AI Use & Governance Policy: SUAPS is developing a formal policy governing the use of AI tools in research, publications, and organizational work — a proactive step appropriate for an organization that takes epistemic responsibility seriously.
  • —Experiencer Policy: all SUAPS projects involving experiencer testimony are required to follow protocols covering informed consent, anonymization, and methodological transparency — a non-negotiable component of the Society's ethical commitment to research subjects.

The goal is not to create bureaucracy for its own sake, but to establish a foundation that supports serious research and educational work over the long term — a trans-generational institutional home for UAP Studies.

08

A Candid Account of Constraints

Honesty with stakeholders requires acknowledging not only what is working but where the organization remains constrained. SUAPS is still young, still lean, and still carrying some of the organizational debts that accumulate when a fast-growing nonprofit advances faster on the ideas-and-programs side than on the administrative side.

  • —Capacity: we are a lean team, and multiple key functions continue to depend on a small number of people. This is not a failure of commitment — it is the structural reality of an early-stage academic nonprofit with an ambitious mission.
  • —Personnel transitions: H1 2026 saw the departure of two significant contributors — our Directors of Digital Communications, and of Education — whose work and institutional knowledge were material assets. Both transitions have been absorbed, but the gaps they created are being actively addressed rather than papered over.
  • —The Lorentz Center outcome: the decline of our Standards of Evidence workshop application was unfortunately but positive, allowing us a much wider international and intellectual scope. We are therefore handling it as an important pivot moment, rather than a closure. Momentum is building, so it is a blessing.
  • —Revenue diversification: our financial position is stable into early next year, and our two recent major donations give us real strategic capacity; but we are actively working to build the diversified revenue base — seminar membership, course tuition, press sales, partnership arrangements — that will make SUAPS resilient to donor concentration risk over the longer term.
09

The Road Ahead — H2 2026 and Beyond

H2 2026 opens with the infrastructure consolidation work substantially complete, a renewed research agenda, and a clear set of programmatic priorities. For stakeholders, the trajectory is forward — deliberate, realistic, and grounded in what the organization actually is and actually has the capacity to do.

Research
All three SIRIs move into active output production — working papers, the Standards of Evidence white paper, and the Limina special issue on the empirical weird.
Seminar Series
August relaunch under Dr. Maya Cowan's leadership, with a refined audience strategy and a cleaner conversion path to membership.
College Credit Course
August marketing campaign launch, January 2027 course start — the first college-credit course in UAP Studies.
Publications
Limina Vol. 3 and the SUAPS Press inaugural release consolidate our publications profile.
Institutional Advancement
Finalizing the Yale MOU and the SCU proceedings collaboration, launching the Responsible Media & Journalism Fellowship, and progressing the Director of Development hire.
Communications
Newsletter reboot, regularized social media cadence, and a website refresh reflecting the current organizational architecture and active project portfolio.

The longer horizon is what drives the work: a trans-generational institutional home for UAP Studies — a place where the most rigorous minds can bring the best tools of scientific inquiry to bear on one of the most persistently strange problems in the history of human observation. We are building it carefully, and we are grateful for the partnership of everyone in this community who has chosen to build it with us.
